hints.junior: About 25% of iPhone users have Reduce Motion enabled. If your animations ignore it, you're running uninvited motion for millions of users.
hints.mid: The safe-by-default pattern wraps animation in prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference. Motion is opt-in: users who haven't set the preference still get it.
hints.staff: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.3.3 is AAA, but SC 2.3.1 (Three Flashes) is A, and flashing animations above 3Hz can trigger seizures. prefers-reduced-motion alone doesn't protect against 2.3.1.

hints.junior: The broken version has full animation with no media query check. The fixed version uses the no-preference guard so animation only runs when reduce motion is not set.
hints.mid: Notice that the fixed version keeps the loading state visible, just without the shimmer animation. Functional information is preserved; decorative motion is removed.
hints.staff: In JavaScript-driven animation (GSAP, Framer Motion), check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches before starting animations, not just CSS.

hints.junior: Picture reduced motion as a light switch, not a dimmer: strategy 1 wraps animation in 'no preference' so it's off unless the switch says otherwise. Strategy 2 does the opposite.
hints.mid: The no-preference guard is the safer default: motion is opt-in, so you can't ship a new animation without automatically getting the protection built in.
hints.staff: Parallax is the highest-risk case — it manufactures a sense of depth the vestibular system contradicts with physical stillness. Anything using translateZ or layered scroll rates needs the no-preference gate, not just an afterthought override.

hints.junior: Read the preference with matchMedia and add a change listener, not just a one-time check on load — someone can toggle Reduce Motion in OS settings while your page is still open.
hints.mid: The change event on the media query fires as soon as the OS setting flips, so you can swap animation states immediately instead of waiting for a reload.
hints.staff: When you strip an animation for reduced-motion users, always land on its end state, not its start state. A collapsed menu should render fully open, not frozen mid-slide — a one-line fix teams miss constantly.

anecdotes.junior: I added a CSS parallax hero to a project. A user with vestibular sensitivity reported it caused dizziness. One media query fixed it; I never skip this check now.
anecdotes.mid: Skeleton shimmers are the most commonly-missed reduce-motion violation. They look subtle but they loop continuously, exactly what vestibular users set the preference to avoid.
anecdotes.staff: Framer Motion's AnimatePresence and animation props accept a 'reduced-motion' mode. Libraries that don't have this require manual window.matchMedia checks.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're honouring the OS 'reduce motion' setting in CSS. It's one media query that prevents real physical harm for users with vestibular conditions.
sections.mid: This case covers prefers-reduced-motion: the two strategies for handling it, the safe-by-default animation pattern, and what to keep vs remove under reduce motion.
sections.staff: We're covering WCAG motion requirements, the no-preference guard pattern, and JavaScript animation library integration for prefers-reduced-motion.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle: read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: The broken version has full animation with no media query check. The fixed version uses the no-preference guard so animation only runs when reduce motion is not set.
sections.mid: Notice that the fixed version keeps the loading state visible, just without the shimmer animation. Functional information is preserved; decorative motion is removed.
sections.staff: In JavaScript-driven animation (GSAP, Framer Motion), check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches before starting animations, not just CSS.
